State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance

• •

Administration

• •

Executive/Legal

• •

Enforcement

• •

Statements of Economic Interest

(916) 322-5662

322-5660

322-5901

322-5441

322-6444

January 6, 1984


Tom Torlakson
Supervisor, District 5
Contra Costa County
3201 St. James Place
Antioch, CA 94509

Re:  Advice Letter No. A-83-273

Dear Mr. Torlakson:

     This letter is to confirm the oral advice that you received
from Robert Leideigh of our office on December 23, 1983.  As we
cannot provide you with advice on your past activities, this
letter discusses only your future actions.  The facts, as stated
in your letter, are that, on January 10, 1984, you will be sworn
in as the chairman of the Board of Supervisors.  You will be
hosting the traditional Oath of Office Day luncheon for Contra
Costa County department heads, staff and friends.  This is not a
fundraising event.  You asked the following questions concerning
the payments for the luncheon:

     (1)  Rather than having each of the 300-400 persons
attending the luncheon send a check to the restaurant, is it
proper to write one check from your campaign committee's
account and have the committee receive the smaller checks?

     (2)  Should an account, other than your campaign
account, be established to make and receive the luncheon
payments?

     (3)  Can a public entity, such as a city or Contra
Costa County, pay for public officials and staff members to
attend the luncheon?

DISCUSSION

     (1)  Your campaign account can write the check for the
luncheon and receive the individual repayments.  The payment for

Tom Torlakson
January 6, 1984
Page 2


the luncheon will be reported as an expenditure on Schedule E of
your campaign statement. The repayments will be reported on
Schedule G, as miscellaneous adjustments to cash.

(2) You may decide to establish a separate account for
making and receiving the payments for the luncheon. This fund
will not constitute a committee and it will not have any
reporting responsibilities under the Political Reform Act.1/

(3) Your question asking whether it is proper for a
governmental entity to pay for its public officials and staff
members to attend the luncheon is outside of the provisions of
the Political Reform Act. You should consult your County
Counsel on this issue.

If you have any additional questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean
Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:plh
Enclosure

_________________________

1/ The fund will not constitute a committee because it
will not receive any contributions. The term "contribution"
only includes payments for which full and adequate consideration
is not received. (Gov. Code Section 82015 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18215, copy enclosed.) In the present case, the
repayments received will cover only the cost of the lunch.

Torlakson
SUPERVISOR '80

November 3, 1985

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 94514


Dear Commissioners:

I am inquiring as to the legality of the City of Pittsburg issuing
a check or the appropriateness of my accepting a check in payment for the
cost of a breakfast event for members of their City Council and staff. The
event was my Oath of Office Breakfast on January 6, 1981.

This function was not a fundraiser but a traditional ceremonial
breakfast for the purpose of laying out my goals and objectives for the office
of Fifth District Supervisor at the outset of my term. In fact, the cost
charged per person did not even cover the actual cost of the event. The
amount of the check which the Citizens for Tom Torlakson Committee account
received from the City of Pittsburg was for $34.50 for six persons.

The campaign checking account was used for convenience sake when
we were told by the Concord Inn that they were not willing to accept a large
number of individual checks. We received approximately 240 small checks for
the event.

Is there anything illegal about accepting this check from the City
of Pittsburg or about the city issuing it? Is this an inadvisable practice
in your opinion under the FPPC guidelines? Would you recommend that the money
be returned to the city?

My wife and I and my office staff at this moment are planning for an
Oath of Office Day lunch for January 10, 1984. I will be sworn in as chairman
of the Board of Supervisors. By tradition the new chair hosts a lunch for
county department heads, staff and friends (which will be paid for by those
who attend). We will again face collecting 300-400 miscellaneous small checks
for a "non-political" event. Do you suggest setting up a separate "committee"
and checking account just for this event, or is it proper to again use our
existing committee's account?

I would appreciate your prompt response to this inquiry. I am
enclosing a news article regarding the event to provide background information.
Please do not hesitate to contact me at (415) 439-4138 for additional infor-
mation.

Sincerely,

Tom Torlakson
Supervisor
District Five

TT:gro

Citizens for Tom Torlakson

3201 St. James Place • Antioch, California 94509 • Telephone (415) 757-2379

TRI-VALLEY

NEWS

Serving Danville, Alamo, Diablo and San Ramon, California • Wednesday, January 7, 1931

Torlakson's call: 'grassroots government'

By Mark Stafiorini
[Article text - appears to continue from Page 1]

Among almost 100 friends, supporters and local dignitaries at an early morning breakfast for incoming Fifth District Supervisor Gene Torlakson at the Osmond, Ill. Gazette, where the new San Ramon Valley representative and his running mates pledged unrelenting dedication to serving the community without the power base that a state politician has had.

Later, after taking the oath of office with re-elected Supervisors Ruth Schader and Nancy Fahner in the board chambers here, Torlakson attended a good-will brunch and said he would propose establishing a task force to study ways to streamlining county services to aid economic development.

"We are one of the most attractive counties in the state," he told the Danville audience, explaining he would pursue a task force to examine county boards' procedures and its many details - an effort said to bring emphasis at least to any new jobs the region's county.

[continuing text about Torlakson's plans and policies...]

Torlakson later told the crowd in a packed board chambers that just as his campaign had been a "grassroots campaign" he urged that there forward to create a "grassroots government."

"I believe the first priority of government is people and the service of those people," he said. "I'd like you to be a part of that process."

Torlakson also advocated a "strengthening stronger" policy of recycling and solid waste. Underlying the state-wide water campaigns and redirecting campaign following stronger air policy during and with the other goals through and [illegible]

[IMAGE CAPTION:]
Delta Justice Court Judge Manuel G. Howe, left, honor Tuesday morning. His wife, Diane, is shown at right as they applauded a tribute to former Supervisor Eric Hasseling, temporarily has the ear of newly elected Supervisor Tom Torlakson during a breakfast in his honor.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a campaign committee can write a single check for an Oath of Office Day luncheon and receive individual reimbursement checks from attendees, whether a separate account should be established for luncheon payments, and whether governmental entities can pay for their officials and staff to attend.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The campaign committee can pay for the luncheon and receive reimbursements, which will be reported as expenditures and miscellaneous adjustments respectively. A separate account may be established for this purpose without triggering committee status or reporting requirements, since reimbursements constitute full consideration. Questions about governmental entity payment require consultation with County Counsel as they fall outside the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (dated November 3, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (dated January 6, 1984). Advice letter confirms prior oral advice. Includes discussion section and footnote explaining that separate fund will not constitute a committee because repayments do not qualify as contributions under Government Code Section 82015.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked the following questions concerning the payments for the luncheon: (1) Rather than having each of the 300-400 persons attending the luncheon send a check to the restaurant, is it proper to write one check from your campaign committee's account and have the committee receive the smaller checks? (2) Should an account, other than your campaign account, be established to make and receive the luncheon payments? (3) Can a public entity, such as a city or Contra Costa County, pay for public officials and staff members to attend the luncheon?

CONCLUSION: Your campaign account can write the check for the luncheon and receive the individual repayments. The payment for the luncheon will be reported as an expenditure on Schedule E of your campaign statement. The repayments will be reported on Schedule G, as miscellaneous adjustments to cash. You may decide to establish a separate account for making and receiving the payments for the luncheon. This fund will not constitute a committee and it will not have any reporting responsibilities under the Political Reform Act. Your question asking whether it is proper for a governmental entity to pay for its public officials and staff members to attend the luncheon is outside of the provisions of the Political Reform Act. You should consult your County Counsel on this issue.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms oral advice regarding Tom Torlakson's Oath of Office Day luncheon planned for January 10, 1984, addressing the legality of using a campaign committee account to collect and receive reimbursement checks from attendees, establishing a separate account, and whether governmental entities can pay for attendance.
